perturbation.original_text: (f) If either Disclosing Party or the Receiving Party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys' fees and costs. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Washington, and the parties further consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal courts sitting in King County, Washington, unless no federal subject matter jurisdiction exists, in which case the parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the Superior of King County, Washington. Company waives all defenses of lack of personal jurisdiction and forum non conveniens. Process may be served on either party in the manner authorized by applicable law or court rule.
perturbation.changed_text: (f) If either Disclosing Party or the Receiving Party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees and costs. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Washington, and the parties further consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal courts sitting in King County, Washington, unless no federal subject matter jurisdiction exists, in which case the parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the Superior of King County, Washington. Company waives all defenses of lack of personal jurisdiction and forum non conveniens. Process may be served on either party in the manner authorized by applicable law or court rule.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the language to stipulate that 'each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees and costs', the contract attempts to waive the prevailing party's right to recover attorneys' fees, potentially contradicting Washington's stance on the recovery of attorney's fees in contract disputes under certain conditions or statutes.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 4.84.330 addresses attorney's fees in contract cases, stating that in any action on a contract providing that attorney's fees and costs, incurred to enforce the provisions of such contract, shall be awarded to one of the parties, the prevailing party, whether that party is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney's fees and costs. The modified text attempts to negate this statutory provision by stating that each party will bear its own fees, regardless of the contract's initial provision or the outcome, which might not be enforceable under Washington law if the original contract contains an attorney fee provision favoring one party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 4.84.330: Actions on contract or lease which provides that attorneys' fees and costs incurred to enforce provisions be awarded to one of parties—Prevailing party entitled to attorneys' fees—Waiver prohibited. Print RCWs > Title 4 > Chapter 4.84 > Section 4.84.330 4.84.320 << 4.84.330 >> 4.84.340 PDF RCW 4.84.330 Actions on contract or lease which provides that attorneys' fees and costs incurred to enforce provisions be awarded to one of parties — Prevailing party entitled to attorneys' fees — Waiver prohibited. In any action on a contract or lease entered into after September 21, 1977, where such contract or lease specifically provides that attorneys' fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce the provisions of such contract or lease, shall be awarded to one of the parties, the prevailing party, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or lease or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees in addition to costs and necessary disbursements. Attorneys' fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract or lease which is entered into after September 21, 1977. Any provision in any such contract or lease which provides for a waiver of attorneys' fees is void. As used in this section "prevailing party" means the party in whose favor final judgment is rendered. [ 2011 c 336 s 131 ; 1977 ex.s. c 203 s 1 .]

Any provision in any such contract or lease which provides for a waiver of attorneys' fees is void. As used in this section "prevailing party" means the party in whose favor fin
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts RCW 4.84.330, which prohibits the waiver of attorney's fees in contracts entered into after September 21, 1977, where the contract specifies that attorney's fees shall be awarded to one party. The original text stipulated that the prevailing party would recover attorney's fees, aligning with the statute. The changed text, however, states that each party shall bear its own fees, effectively waiving the prevailing party's right to recover fees. This directly contradicts the law snippet's provision that "Attorneys' fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract... Any provision in any such contract or lease which provides for a waiver of attorneys' fees is void."

perturbation.original_text: (b) In the event that the Disclosing Party provides any computer software and/or hardware to the Receiving Party as Confidential Information under the terms of this Agreement, such computer software and/or hardware may only be used by the Receiving Party for evaluation and providing Feedback (as defined in Section 5 of this Agreement) to the Disclosing Party. Unless otherwise agreed by the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party, all such computer software and/or hardware is provided "AS IS" without warranty of any kind, and Receiving Party agrees that neither Disclosing Party nor its suppliers shall be liable for any damages whatsoever arising from or relating to Receiving Party's use or inability to use such software and/or hardware.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) In the event that the Disclosing Party provides any computer software and/or hardware to the Receiving Party as Confidential Information under the terms of this Agreement, such computer software and/or hardware may only be used by the Receiving Party for evaluation and providing Feedback (as defined in Section 5 of this Agreement) to the Disclosing Party. All such computer software and/or hardware is provided "AS IS" without warranty of any kind, and Receiving Party agrees that neither Disclosing Party nor its suppliers shall be liable for any damages whatsoever arising from or relating to Receiving Party's use or inability to use such software and/or hardware.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "Unless otherwise agreed by the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party,", the contract removes the possibility of a warranty which could be interpreted as a violation of implied warranty laws, particularly under the UCC as adopted by Washington State, if the goods provided are not fit for their ordinary purpose and a warranty is implied.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Implied Warranty of Merchantability (RCW 62A.2-314)
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 62A.2-314 establishes the implied warranty of merchantability. Removing the clause that allows for an alternative agreement potentially violates this law, because now every agreement automatically uses the AS IS statement, even when a warranty should be implied by default. This shifts all risk onto the Receiving Party, potentially voiding consumer protections related to the fitness and usability of provided hardware and software, and it does so without explicit consent or a clear alternative.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 62A.2-314: Implied warranty: Merchantability; usage of trade. Print RCWs > Title 62A > Chapter 62A.2 > Section 62A.2-314 62A.2-313 << 62A.2-314 >> 62A.2-315 PDF RCW 62A.2-314 Implied warranty: Merchantability; usage of trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (RCW 62A.2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (RCW 62A.2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. [1965 ex.s. c 157 s 2-314. Cf. former RCW 63.04.160 (2); 1925 ex.s. c 142 s 15 ; RRS s 5836-15.]
perturbation.justification: The omission of "Unless otherwise agreed by the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party," directly contradicts the clause that enables modification or exclusion of implied warranties as stated in RCW 62A.2-314. By removing this clause, the agreement now defaults to an 'AS IS' condition without allowing for any potential warranty agreements, potentially violating the Receiving Party's implied warranty rights.

perturbation.original_text: (c) The parties agree to comply with all applicable international and national laws that apply to (i) any Confidential Information, or (ii) any product (or any part thereof), process or service that is the direct product of the Confidential Information, including the U.S. Export Administration Regulations, as well as end-user, end-use and destination restrictions issued by U.S. and other governments. For additional information on exporting Microsoft products, see http://www.microsoft.com/exporting/.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) The parties agree to comply with all applicable international laws that apply to (i) any Confidential Information, or (ii) any product (or any part thereof), process or service that is the direct product of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'national laws...including the U.S. Export Administration Regulations, as well as end-user, end-use and destination restrictions issued by U.S. and other governments,' the modified text creates a potential legal contradiction with U.S. export control laws if confidential information or products derived from it are exported without adhering to these regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR), codified in 15 CFR Parts 730-774, govern the export and re-export of certain goods, software, and technology. By omitting the explicit reference to compliance with U.S. export regulations, the contract could be interpreted as neglecting a crucial aspect of legal compliance, particularly if the confidential information relates to controlled technologies. This omission introduces a legal risk if the company exports items without adhering to EAR restrictions, potentially leading to fines or other penalties. Note that both companies are based in the USA.

Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes explicit compliance with U.S. national laws, specifically the U.S. Export Administration Regulations (EAR). The original text explicitly mentions compliance with these regulations, while the modified version only refers to 'all applicable international laws'. Since both companies are based in the USA, the EAR is highly relevant. The omission directly contradicts the need to comply with U.S. export regulations if confidential information or products derived from it are exported. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly refers to the EAR under 15 CFR 730-774, indicating the importance of these regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 further reinforces this by referencing Subchapter C of Title 15, dedicated to Export Administration Regulations. The omission therefore creates a significant legal loophole.

perturbation.original_text: (i) Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to the other party. All sections of this Agreement relating to the rights and obligations of the parties concerning Confidential Information disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive any such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: (i) Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the provision that 'All sections of this Agreement relating to the rights and obligations of the parties concerning Confidential Information disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive any such termination,' the modified text contradicts established trade secret law, potentially violating the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (WUTSA) regarding the misappropriation of trade secrets even after the termination of an agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (WUTSA) protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Even after termination of an agreement, the obligation to protect trade secrets generally continues. By removing the clause that explicitly states the confidentiality obligations survive termination, the contract implies that these obligations cease upon termination, which would conflict with WUTSA. This places the Disclosing Party at risk of trade secret misappropriation without clear contractual recourse after the agreement is terminated.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Chapter 19.108 RCW: UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 Complete Chapter HTML PDF | RCW Dispositions Chapter 19.108 RCW UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Sections HTML PDF 19.108.010 Definitions. HTML PDF 19.108.020 Remedies for misappropriation — Injunction, royalty. HTML PDF 19.108.030 Remedies for misappropriation — Damages. HTML PDF 19.108.040 Award of attorney's fees. HTML PDF 19.108.050 Court orders to preserve secrecy of alleged trade secrets. HTML PDF 19.108.060 Actions for misappropriation — Time limitation. HTML PDF 19.108.900 Effect of chapter on other law. HTML PDF 19.108.910 Construction of uniform act. HTML PDF 19.108.920 Short title. HTML PDF 19.108.930 Effective date — Application — 1981 c 286. NOTES: Requiring assignment of employee's rights to inventions: RCW 49.44.140 , 49.44.150 .
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause regarding the survival of confidentiality obligations after termination directly contradicts the intent and potential enforcement of trade secret protection under the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (WUTSA). While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly references the "UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT" and lists sections covering remedies for misappropriation and injunctions, implying a legal basis for continued protection even after contract termination. The omission weakens the disclosing party's ability to enforce trade secret protection after the agreement ends, conflicting with WUTSA's aim to prevent misappropriation. The changed text creates a legal loophole where confidentiality obligations could be interpreted as ceasing upon termination, undermining the protection intended by trade secret law.
